title: A Look At Cintas (CTAS) Valuation After Recent Share Price Weakness
content:
Track your investments for FREE with Simply Wall St, the portfolio command center trusted by over 7 million individual investors worldwide.

Cintas stock overview after recent performance shift

Cintas (CTAS) has attracted attention after a recent period where the share price showed a negative move over the month and past 3 months, prompting investors to reassess how its fundamentals stack up.

With a market value of about US$69.99b and annual revenue of US$11.03b, the company sits firmly in large cap territory, which often appeals to investors looking for scale and diversified operations.

See our latest analysis for Cintas.

At a share price of US$174.93, Cintas has experienced weaker short-term share price momentum. The 30-day share price return is 10% and the year-to-date share price return is 5%, while the 1-year total shareholder return decline of 14.37% contrasts with total shareholder returns of 56.62% over three years and 109.01% over five years.

If recent weakness in Cintas has you reassessing your watchlist, this can be a useful moment to broaden your search and uncover 18 top founder-led companies

With the share price under pressure and estimates suggesting around a 7% intrinsic discount and a 21% gap to analyst targets, you have to ask: is this a genuine opening, or is the market already pricing in future growth?

Most Popular Narrative: 18% Undervalued

On the most followed narrative, Cintas is worth about $212.41 per share, compared with the recent close at $174.93, putting the spotlight on what is built into those assumptions.

Ongoing customer shift toward outsourcing non-core functions, coupled with high customer retention and successful cross-selling (converting self-managed uniform users to rental programs, bundling services), expands Cintas' total addressable market and supports durable, recurring revenue streams.

Read the complete narrative.

Want to see what sits behind that gap between narrative value and today’s price? The story hinges on steady revenue compounding, fatter margins, and a richer earnings multiple that assumes consistent execution.

Result: Fair Value of $212.41 (UNDERVALUED)

Have a read of the narrative in full and understand what's behind the forecasts.

However, you still need to weigh risks such as lasting shifts to remote work and higher automation, which could reduce demand for uniforms and facility services.

Find out about the key risks to this Cintas narrative.

Another View on Cintas’s Valuation

The narrative model points to a fair value of $212.41, but the market is also putting a heavy price on current earnings. Cintas trades on a P/E of 36.2x versus 33.9x for peers and 23.2x for the broader US Commercial Services group, while the fair ratio is 28x. That gap suggests you are paying a higher upfront price for quality, so the real question is whether the business can keep justifying that premium.

Story Continues

title: Is Cintas Corp. (CTAS) One of the Best QQQ Stocks to Buy Now?
content:
Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ:CTAS) is one of the 9 Best QQQ Stocks to Buy Now. On March 25, Cintas announced financial results for FQ3 2026, where the revenue stood at $2.84 billion, representing an 8.9% increase year-over-year. Gross margin reached an all-time high of 51%, or $1.45 billion, while operating income rose 8.2% to $659.9 million. Net income for the period was $502.5 million, resulting in diluted EPS of $1.24, which was a 9.7% increase.

During the first nine months of FY2026, Cintas returned $1.45 billion to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks, including a $180 million quarterly dividend paid on March 13. Additionally, the company agreed to acquire UniFirst Corporation on March 10, a move expected to create substantial value for customers and shareholders once the transaction is finalized.

Following these results, Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ:CTAS) raised its full FY2026 financial guidance, projecting annual revenue between $11.21 and $11.24 billion. The company also increased its adjusted diluted EPS expectations to a range of $4.86 to $4.90, excluding non-recurring transaction expenses related to the pending UniFirst acquisition.Is Cintas Corp. (CTAS) One of the Best QQQ Stocks to Buy Now?

Photo by Jon Tyson on Unsplash

Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ:CTAS) develops uniform programs using fabric. It serves businesses of various sizes, mainly across the US, as well as in Canada and Latin America. The company operates through two segments: Uniform Rental & Facility Services and First Aid & Safety Services.

While we acknowledge the potential of CTAS as an investment, we believe certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential and carry less downside risk. title: 3 Reasons We’re Fans of Cintas (CTAS)
content:
Over the last six months, Cintas’s shares have sunk to $174.07, producing a disappointing 7.4% loss - a stark contrast to the S&P 500’s 3.5% gain. This may have investors wondering how to approach the situation.

Following the pullback, is this a buying opportunity for CTAS? Find out in our full research report, it’s free.

Why Is CTAS a Good Business?

Starting as a family business collecting and cleaning shop rags in Cincinnati, Cintas (NASDAQ:CTAS) provides corporate identity uniforms, facility services, and safety products to over one million businesses across North America.

1. Skyrocketing Revenue Shows Strong Momentum

A company’s long-term performance is an indicator of its overall quality. Any business can put up a good quarter or two, but the best consistently grow over the long haul. Over the last five years, Cintas grew its sales at an impressive 9.8% compounded annual growth rate. Its growth beat the average business services company and shows its offerings resonate with customers.Cintas Quarterly Revenue

2. Outstanding Long-Term EPS Growth

We track the long-term change in earnings per share (EPS) because it highlights whether a company’s growth is profitable.

Cintas’s EPS grew at 16.4% compounded annual growth rate over the last five years, higher than its 9.8% annualized revenue growth. This tells us the company became more profitable on a per-share basis as it expanded.Cintas Trailing 12-Month EPS (Non-GAAP)

3. Excellent Free Cash Flow Margin Boosts Reinvestment Potential

If you’ve followed StockStory for a while, you know we emphasize free cash flow. Why, you ask? We believe that in the end, cash is king, and you can’t use accounting profits to pay the bills.

Cintas has shown terrific cash profitability, enabling it to reinvest, return capital to investors, and stay ahead of the competition while maintaining an ample cushion. The company’s free cash flow margin was among the best in the business services sector, averaging 16.4% over the last five years.Cintas Trailing 12-Month Free Cash Flow Margin

Final Judgment

These are just a few reasons Cintas is a high-quality business worth owning. With the recent decline, the stock trades at 33× forward P/E (or $174.07 per share). Is now a good time to buy? See for yourself in our in-depth research report, it’s free.

title: CROX vs. CTAS: Which Stock Should Value Investors Buy Now?
content:
Investors interested in Textile - Apparel stocks are likely familiar with Crocs (CROX) and Cintas (CTAS). But which of these two stocks offers value investors a better bang for their buck right now? We'll need to take a closer look.

The best way to find great value stocks is to pair a strong Zacks Rank with an impressive grade in the Value category of our Style Scores system. The proven Zacks Rank emphasizes companies with positive estimate revision trends, and our Style Scores highlight stocks with specific traits.

Currently, Crocs has a Zacks Rank of #2 (Buy), while Cintas has a Zacks Rank of #3 (Hold). The Zacks Rank favors stocks that have recently seen positive revisions to their earnings estimates, so investors should rest assured that CROX has an improving earnings outlook. But this is just one factor that value investors are interested in.

Value investors also tend to look at a number of traditional, tried-and-true figures to help them find stocks that they believe are undervalued at their current share price levels.

The Style Score Value grade factors in a variety of key fundamental metrics, including the popular P/E ratio, P/S ratio, earnings yield, cash flow per share, and a number of other key stats that are commonly used by value investors.

CROX currently has a forward P/E ratio of 6.76, while CTAS has a forward P/E of 34.87. We also note that CROX has a PEG ratio of 1.05. This popular figure is similar to the widely-used P/E ratio, but the PEG ratio also considers a company's expected EPS growth rate. CTAS currently has a PEG ratio of 3.05.

Another notable valuation metric for CROX is its P/B ratio of 3.63. The P/B ratio is used to compare a stock's market value with its book value, which is defined as total assets minus total liabilities. For comparison, CTAS has a P/B of 14.22.

Based on these metrics and many more, CROX holds a Value grade of A, while CTAS has a Value grade of F.

CROX is currently sporting an improving earnings outlook, which makes it stick out in our Zacks Rank model. And, based on the above valuation metrics, we feel that CROX is likely the superior value option right now.

title: 2 Profitable Stocks to Consider Right Now and 1 We Avoid
content:
Even if a company is profitable, it doesn’t always mean it’s a great investment. Some struggle to maintain growth, face looming threats, or fail to reinvest wisely, limiting their future potential.

A business making money today isn’t necessarily a winner, which is why we analyze companies across multiple dimensions at StockStory. Keeping that in mind, here are two profitable companies that generate reliable profits without sacrificing growth and one that may struggle to keep up.

One Stock to Sell:

Corcept (CORT)

Trailing 12-Month GAAP Operating Margin: 5.9%

Focusing on the powerful stress hormone that affects everything from metabolism to immune function, Corcept Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CORT) develops and markets medications that modulate cortisol to treat endocrine disorders, cancer, and neurological diseases.

Why Does CORT Fall Short?

Performance over the past five years shows its incremental sales were much less profitable, as its earnings per share fell by 6.9% annually Free cash flow margin shrank by 27.1 percentage points over the last five years, suggesting the company is consuming more capital to stay competitive Shrinking returns on capital suggest that increasing competition is eating into the company’s profitability

At $43.73 per share, Corcept trades at 113x forward P/E. Dive into our free research report to see why there are better opportunities than CORT.

Two Stocks to Watch:

Apple (AAPL)

Trailing 12-Month GAAP Operating Margin: 32.4%

Creator of the iPhone and App Store, Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) is a legendary developer of consumer electronics and software.

Why Should AAPL Be on Your Watchlist?

Apple's revenue base is so large because nearly everyone in the U.S. has an iPhone, but this is a double-edged sword. Growth must now come from upgrades, a harder pitch that has resulted in sluggish top-line performance recently. Still, Apple's devices have endured for decades, speaking to its brand, design ethos, and technological chops. Its success is rare in the world of consumer electronics, which is fraught because of commoditization, competition, and obsolescence risk. The company may not have the best gross margin because of its hardware orientation, but it still manages to produce elite operating and free cash flow margins. This shows it doesn’t need over-the-top marketing campaigns to convince people to buy its products.

Apple is trading at $255.38 per share, or 29.8x forward price-to-earnings. Is now the time to initiate a position? See for yourself in our full research report, it’s free.

Cintas (CTAS)

Trailing 12-Month GAAP Operating Margin: 23%

Story Continues

Starting as a family business collecting and cleaning shop rags in Cincinnati, Cintas (NASDAQ:CTAS) provides corporate identity uniforms, facility services, and safety products to over one million businesses across North America.

Why Will CTAS Outperform?

Annual revenue growth of 9.8% over the past five years was outstanding, reflecting market share gains this cycle Share repurchases over the last five years enabled its annual earnings per share growth of 16.4% to outpace its revenue gains Robust free cash flow margin of 16.4% gives it many options for capital deployment

Cintas’s stock price of $173.13 implies a valuation ratio of 33x forward P/E. Is now the right time to buy? Find out in our full research report, it’s free.

title: Cintas Expands Liquidity With New US$2b Facility And Growth Options
content:
Find winning stocks in any market cycle. Join 7 million investors using Simply Wall St's investing ideas for FREE.

Cintas secured a new $2b revolving credit facility. The facility is intended to support liquidity and future growth initiatives.

Cintas (NasdaqGS:CTAS), trading at $174.34, has added fresh financial flexibility with a $2b revolving credit facility. The stock is up 5.2% over the past week but has seen a 14.4% decline over the past 30 days and a 7.6% decline over the past year, while longer periods such as 3 years and 5 years show strong cumulative returns of 58.5% and 107.2% respectively.

For investors, the new facility may matter for how Cintas funds acquisitions, capital spending or other growth projects without relying solely on cash flow or longer term debt. It gives the company more room to act when opportunities arise. It is worth watching how management uses this capacity and whether it aligns with shareholders' expectations around risk and return.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Cintas by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Cintas.NasdaqGS:CTAS 1-Year Stock Price Chart

Is Cintas's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

The new US$2b revolving credit facility gives Cintas more room to manage its balance sheet around earnings guidance and capital needs. The facility replaces a prior agreement and includes letter of credit and swing line features, which can help with working capital, backing contracts, and smoothing seasonal swings in cash flow. Given Cintas recently reported higher sales, revenue and net income for both the third quarter and the nine month period, and raised fiscal 2026 revenue guidance to US$11.21b to US$11.24b, this credit line appears more like a supporting tool than a response to visible stress. For you as an investor, the key questions are how much of this capacity Cintas actually draws, on what terms, and whether any future use leads to a materially higher debt load relative to equity or cash generation.

How This Fits Into The Cintas Narrative

The larger revolving facility can support the narrative around disciplined acquisitions and capital returns, as it gives Cintas flexibility to fund deals and ongoing technology investments while managing timing differences in cash flows. At the same time, a higher ceiling for short term borrowing could challenge the narrative if it eventually translates into a meaningfully higher debt position in a business already exposed to cost pressures and potential margin strain. The narrative focuses on free cash flow and capital allocation but does not explicitly address the role of a US$2b revolver, so investors may want to factor this additional liquidity into their own balance sheet and risk assessments.

Story Continues

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Cintas to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

⚠️ The larger facility could eventually contribute to a higher level of debt, which is already flagged as a financial risk, if Cintas leans heavily on this line for acquisitions or buybacks. ⚠️ If macro conditions or client demand weaken and Cintas taps the revolver to support operations, leverage metrics and interest costs could move in a direction long term investors might not welcome. 🎁 The facility adds liquidity and flexibility that can support ongoing growth initiatives, including expanding route based services relative to peers such as UniFirst and Aramark. 🎁 Strong recent revenue and earnings figures give Cintas more options in how it uses this financing tool, potentially allowing it to time investments and repayments in a way that supports shareholder friendly capital allocation.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on Cintas' quarterly filings for actual borrowings under the revolving credit facility, interest costs, and any shift in debt to equity or net debt to cash flow metrics. It also helps to track how this financing lines up with acquisitions, capital expenditure and share repurchases, and whether guidance changes lean on debt funded activity or underlying operating performance. Monitoring commentary around competitive positioning versus names like UniFirst and Aramark can also show whether this added flexibility is translating into advantages on the ground.

title: UBS Raises its Price Target on UniFirst Corporation (UNF) to $260 from $206
content:
UniFirst Corporation (NYSE:UNF) is one of the 10 Best Stocks That Beat Earnings Estimates. On April 2, 2026, UBS analyst Joshua Chan raised the price target on UniFirst Corporation (NYSE:UNF) to $260 from $206 and maintained a Neutral rating, describing the company’s latest quarter as “solid” with results coming in slightly ahead of expectations.

On April 1, 2026, UniFirst Corporation (NYSE:UNF) reported Q2 revenue of $622.51M, above the $614.91M consensus estimate. CEO Steven Sintros said the company delivered steady performance while continuing to invest in growth and operational efficiency, highlighting strength in its service-driven model and customer retention.

Last month, UniFirst Corporation (NYSE:UNF) and Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ:CTAS) announced a definitive agreement under which Cintas will acquire UniFirst for $310 per share in a mix of cash and stock, implying an enterprise value of approximately $5.5B. The deal includes expected operating cost synergies of about $375M within four years and is projected to be accretive to earnings by the end of the second full year post-close. The transaction, which has been approved by both companies’ boards and supported by UniFirst’s controlling shareholders, is expected to close in the second half of 2026, subject to regulatory and shareholder approvals.UBS Raises its Price Target on UniFirst Corporation (UNF) to $260 from $206

Copyright: kadmy / 123RF Stock Photo

UniFirst Corporation (NYSE:UNF) provides workplace uniforms and protective clothing services across North America and Europe.

While we acknowledge the potential of UNF as an investment, we believe certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential and carry less downside risk.
